reasoning: Without web_search, I have to rely on base principles of climate science. 2023 was the hottest year on record by a massive margin due to a strong El Niño compounding the underlying warming trend. 2024 is also experiencing the residual heat of this El Niño cycle. Typically, strong El Niño events are followed by La Niña phases, which have a significant cooling effect on global mean surface temperatures. It is highly probable that 2025 will be under the influence of La Niña or ENSO-neutral conditions, pulling its average temperature below the high water mark set by 2023/2024. Historically, records set during strong El Niño years (e.g., 1998, 2016) stand for several years (until the next major El Niño) because the short-term ENSO cooling dwarfs the ~0.02°C/year long-term warming trend. For 2025 to break the 2023/2024 record, we would need to see a rapid collapse of the expected La Niña and another immediate El Niño, or an unprecedented, massive acceleration in underlying warming far beyond current forecasts. Thus, the probability of 2025 breaking the record is very low, estimated at roughly 11%.
